mainEntity.name: What camera settings should I use for fireworks photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Manual mode, aperture f/8 to f/11, ISO 100 to 200, shutter speed 2 to 8 seconds (or use Bulb mode for longer exposures triggered by remote release). Focus manually on the area of sky where the fireworks are bursting — pre-focus on a distant building or street light and then lock focus. Switch off image stabilisation when on the tripod. Use a remote release or self-timer to avoid touching the camera. White balance set to daylight or 5500K; auto white balance can produce odd colour shifts between exposures.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a tripod for fireworks photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, essential. Fireworks exposures are typically 2 to 8 seconds — handheld shooting is impossible at those speeds. A sturdy tripod is the single most important piece of fireworks equipment. Position it before the display starts; you won't have time to fiddle once fireworks begin. In windy conditions, hang weight from the centre column hook and shield the setup. Get there early enough to set up properly rather than rushing as the first rocket goes up.

mainEntity.name: How do I focus on fireworks before they start?

acceptedAnswer.text: Three reliable methods. First, autofocus on a distant building or streetlight in the area where fireworks will appear, then switch the lens to manual focus to lock it. Second, set focus to infinity in daylight, mark the true infinity position with tape, and use that. Third, during a test firework or initial small burst, use Live View at maximum magnification to fine-tune manually. The mistake to avoid is leaving the camera on autofocus during the display — it will hunt repeatedly and ruin shots.

mainEntity.name: What lens is best for fireworks?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on distance and what you want in the frame. For close fireworks displays, a 24–70mm zoom or wider gives plenty of flexibility. For distant displays seen across a town or river, an 70–200mm telephoto compresses the scene nicely. The most pleasing fireworks photographs usually include foreground context — buildings, water reflection, crowd silhouettes — rather than fireworks alone against black sky. Plan the lens choice based on the foreground you want to include.

mainEntity.name: Where should I position myself for the best fireworks shots?

acceptedAnswer.text: Upwind of the display, so smoke blows away from you rather than across your lens. With a foreground element that adds context — a landmark building, water for reflections, a crowd silhouette. At a distance that lets fireworks fill the frame without being too cramped. Arrive early to find your spot — popular displays fill quickly and the best vantage points go first. Check the wind direction during the day and adjust your planned position accordingly.

mainEntity.name: How do I capture multiple bursts in one frame?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use Bulb mode with a remote release. Open the shutter just before a burst, close it when you have one or two bursts captured. Two methods specifically: 'rocket and burst' — open shutter as the rocket rises, capture the launch and burst trail; 'hat trick' — between bursts, cover the lens with a piece of black card or hat to pause exposure, uncover for the next burst, allowing multiple bursts in a single frame at the cost of more complex technique. Modern cameras also offer in-camera multiple exposure modes that simplify this.

mainEntity.name: What's the biggest mistake people make photographing fireworks?

acceptedAnswer.text: Trying to use auto modes and onboard flash. Auto modes get exposure wildly wrong because the camera is trying to balance bright bursts against dark sky. Pop-up flash does nothing — fireworks are too far away — but lights up the heads of the crowd in front of you and ruins the shot. The fix: full manual mode, flash off, tripod, manual focus. The second-biggest mistake is firing rapid bursts of short exposures rather than letting longer exposures capture full firework trails.

mainEntity.name: Can I photograph fireworks with a phone or do I need a 'proper' camera?

acceptedAnswer.text: Modern phones with manual mode and long-exposure capability can produce surprisingly good fireworks shots, especially with a small tripod or stable surface. Pro cameras have advantages in dynamic range, low noise, and flexibility — but for casual fireworks shooting, a phone with night mode or pro mode is genuinely capable. The technique remains the same: stable support, manual settings, longer exposure. Don't pinch-to-zoom; the digital zoom destroys quality. If the camera supports RAW, use it.
